system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

### 1. Read and Understand First", "new_string": "## Pattern: Content-Driven Page Expansion (standalone sites)

When adding pages to a standalone Next.js site that uses `content/es.json` (or similar):

1. **First expand content.json** — add all new page data in a single edit before touching any page files. Each page gets its own top-level key in the JSON.
2. **Create all pages in parallel** — write every `/app/<page>/page.tsx` at once. Each imports `@/content/es.json` and reads its section.
3. **Then update shared components** — Header, Footer, globals.css in a single batch.
4. **Build once** — `npm run build` to catch all errors at once.

Key conventions for 10-page sites:
- Put ALL content in es.json — zero hardcoded text in page components. This lets non-developers edit content without touching code.
- Use `"use client"` on every page since content.json import forces client-side rendering.
- Batch page creation as write_file calls, not one-at-a-time.
- Add a "Más ▾" dropdown in the desktop nav when links exceed 5, with a hamburger for mobile.
- Import `content` as a module-level variable: `import content from "@/content/es.json"`.
- Use `c` as local alias: `const c = content.pageSection`.
- Contact forms should submit to WhatsApp via `window.open(wa.me/...)` — no backend needed.

Build fails with: ``` Export Facebook doesn't exist in target module ``` **Fix:** Use inline SVG components instead of lucide imports for social icons. Keep lucide for standard icons (Menu, X, ChevronDown, etc.) which are still available. Example inline Instagram SVG: ```tsx function InstagramIcon({ size = 20 }: { size?: number }) { return ( ) } ``` Check lucide version before importing: `cat node_modules/lucide-react/package.json | grep '"version"'`. Anything 1.14+ drops social icons. ### 🔥 Next.js standalone `output: "standalone"` May Cause 404 on Production When `next.config.js` sets `output: "standalone"`, the built server may return `Cannot GET /` 404 for ALL routes in production. This happens with `next start` (the `CMD` in Dockerfile). The standalone server (server.js) expects a specific directory structure that may not match the build output. **Symptom:** `curl -s http://localhost:3000/` returns `Cannot GET /` but logging shows `✓ Ready`. The `.next/server/app/index.html` exists on disk but the server doesn't serve it. **Working pattern — skip standalone output, use `next start` directly:** ```js // next.config.js — do NOT set output: "standalone" module.exports = {} ``` Then in Dockerfile, run `npx next start` (not the standalone server.js): ```Dockerfile COPY --from=builder /app/next.config.js ./ COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next ./.next COPY --from=builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules COPY --from=builder /app/content ./content CMD ["npx", "next", "start"] ``` If the build uses Turbopack (Next.js 15+), the standalone output also fails because Turbopack's generated server.js has a different entry point. Always build without Turbopack for production deployments. ### 🔥 Docker Swarm "Insufficient Resources" on Single-Node VPS When deploying a new standalone client with `docker stack deploy`, the default `replicas: 2` often fails with: ``` no suitable node (insufficient resources on 1 node) ``` This happens on the single-node VPS (72.61.44.159) when memory is already consumed by the 15+ other services (Traefik, cron jobs, Hermes, all client sites). **Fix:** Scale down to 1 replica after deploy: **Fallback — `docker run` when Swarm fails to schedule:** If `service update` or `scale` repeatedly shows `Pending` with `no suitable node (insufficient resources)`, check: 1. Disk: `df -h /var/lib/docker` — if >75%, run `docker builder prune -af` (155GB+ build cache is common) 2. Port conflict: `ss -tlnp | grep ':3000'` — kill stale node processes from old containers 3. Swarm overload: 20+ services on a single node can cause the scheduler to stall If Swarm won't schedule after clearing both, deploy as standalone container: ```bash docker service rm {stack}_web docker run -d --restart unless-stopped --name {client}-web \ --network agent-net \ -l traefik.enable=true \ -l traefik.http.routers.{router}.entrypoints=websecure \ -l traefik.http.routers.{router}.rule="Host(\`{domain}\`)" \ -l traefik.http.routers.{router}.tls=true \ -l traefik.http.routers.{router}.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver \ -l traefik.http.services.{router}.loadbalancer.server.port=3000 \ -l traefik.docker.network=agent-net \ {image}:prod ``` This bypasses the Swarm scheduler entirely. Traefik discovers the container via Docker labels. No port binding needed — Traefik uses the overlay network. ```bash docker service scale {stack}_web=1 ``` Or set `replicas: 1` in docker-compose.yml before first deploy. **Verification:** After scaling, check the new task starts and the old one (Shutdown) is replaced: ```bash docker service ps {stack}_web --format "{{.Name}} {{.CurrentState}}" ``` **Impact:** No performance loss — 1 replica on a single-node VPS handles all traffic. The second replica would just sit on the same node anyway with no HA benefit. ### 🔥 Docker Swarm Local Image Tagging — image:latest Not :prod When building a local image for Swarm deployment, the `docker stack deploy` command expects the image tag specified in `docker-compose.yml`. If `image: brahm-the-racoon:latest` is specified but the image was built as `brahm-the-racoon:prod`, Swarm reports `"No such image: brahm-the-racoon:latest"`. **Fix — tag the local image to match compose:** ```bash docker build -t {client}:prod . docker tag {client}:prod {client}:latest # Swarm needs :latest or match compose exactly docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml {client} ``` Or change compose to use `image: {client}:prod` directly. The tagged image must be on the node before stack deploy — Swarm won't pull local images from other nodes. ### 🔥 Remove ports: Mapping When Using Traefik Overlay Network When Traefik routes traffic via the `agent-net` overlay network, **remove the `ports:` mapping** from docker-compose.yml. The `ports:` mapping binds the container port to the host, which is unnecessary and can cause port conflicts (multiple clients all trying to publish port 3000). Correct compose pattern: ```yaml services: web: image: {client}:latest environment: - NODE_ENV=production deploy: replicas: 2 labels: - "traefik.enable=true" - "traefik.http.routers.{router}.entrypoints=websecure" - "traefik.http.routers.{router}.rule=Host(`{domain}`)" - "traefik.http.routers.{router}.tls=true" - "traefik.http.routers.{router}.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver" - "traefik.http.services.{router}.loadbalancer.server.port=3000" - "traefik.docker.network=agent-net" networks: - agent-net networks: agent-net: external: true ``` No `ports:` section — Traefik discovers the service via Docker labels on the overlay network. ### 🔥 PAB Token Pattern for Standalone Sites The PAB design-tokens JSON pattern (`src/tokens/{client}.tokens.json`) works standalone for non-PAB sites. The tokens file serves as documentation/source-of-truth — not consumed by a token resolver at build time (unlike the PAB engine which uses `resolve-site-tokens.ts`). ### 🔥 Business-Plan-to-Website Pattern (Zero-Code Repos) When the client has a repo with ONLY business docs (research, menus, branding, ops checklists) and ZERO website code: 1. **Inventory the repo**: find all `.md` files in `menu/`, `branding/`, `website/`, `business-plan/` 2. **Extract structured data into `content/es.json`**: - Menu items with prices → `menu.categories[].items[]` - Brand colors → CSS `@theme` in globals.css - Brand voice/tone → copy for hero and sections - Location/hours → `ubicacion` section 3. **Every page, component, and CSS file must be written from scratch** — there's nothing to copy or modify 4. **Reference the dayah-litworks pattern** for file structure (layout.tsx, page structure, Dockerfile, docker-compose.yml) 5. **Content JSON is hand-crafted** — not extracted from PAB, so no PAB path stripping needed 6. **Deploy as a new standalone client** — same Docker + Traefik pattern as all other clients 7. **The business-plan repo itself becomes the website repo** — force push to replace the old docs with the new code (after user confirms) See `references/business-plan-to-website-pattern.md` for the Stroopwafel Huis session as reference. ### 🔥 GitHub Packages Auth Broken — npm install @ai-whisperers/* Fails The `.npmrc` with `@ai-whisperers:registry=https://npm.pkg.github.com/` works for displaying package info but `npm install` fails with `401 Unauthorized`. Fix: `docker service scale paragu-ai-builder_web=1 && sleep 15 && docker service scale paragu-ai-builder_web=3` to explicitly kill all old replicas. Run `docker service logs paragu-ai-builder_web --tail 5 | grep -iE \"Ready|error|ReferenceError\"` to confirm no stale instances are still receiving traffic. If an old instance with a ReferenceError keeps appearing in logs, the load balancer is still routing to it — the scale-down didn't fully evict it. Force with `docker service update --force --image paragu-ai:prod paragu-ai-builder_web`. **Deploy trap — `docker service update --force` does NOT rebuild the image.** It only restarts containers with the EXISTING image tag. Test by checking `docker service logs paragu-ai-builder_web --tail 5 | grep "ReferenceError"` after deploy — if present, the fix is to patch both locations and redeploy. ## Subagent Page Imports Out of Sync (Expanded) When using `delegate_task` to create page files, the subagent may use different import paths or component names than actual files on disk. **This happened in BOTH dayah and elviajero builds** — the subagent created `.js` files instead of `.tsx`, imported `@/components/faq-section` instead of `@/components/faq`, and left `Header.js`/`Footer.js`/`WhatsAppFloat.js` alongside the `.tsx` versions. **Verification command after ANY subagent page creation:** ```bash # 1. The sequence: deploy looks successful (no errors), service shows 2/2 replicas, overlay test returns 200 — but external HTTPS returns 000. Debug checklist: 1. **Check Traefik labels are applied:** `docker service inspect {stack_name}_web --format '{{json .Spec.TaskTemplate.ContainerSpec.Labels}}' | python3 -m json.tool` — if only `com.docker.stack.namespace` is present, the labels were NOT applied. This happens when `docker stack deploy` receives a compose file with `build:` instead of `image:` — it silently drops `deploy.labels` too. 2. **Check SSL cert status:** `docker service logs traefik_traefik --tail 20 2>&1 | grep -i '{domain}'` — if you see `rateLimited`, Let's Encrypt has banned the domain for ~1h due to too many failed authorization attempts. Cloudflare proxy mode + LetsEncrypt HTTP-01 = the challenge hits Cloudflare's IP, not the origin, causing repeated failures. 3. **Stack name collision:** If a client was deployed with different stack names (e.g. `alejandro_villayamor` then `alejandro`), the OLD Traefik service discovery still has the old stack's container info. `docker stack rm {old_stack}` resolves it. Always remove ALL prior stacks before redeploying a migrated client. 4. **Certificate verification lag:** Even after Traefik issues a cert, it can take 30-60 seconds for Cloudflare's edge to cache the new cert. The initial requests hit 526 while propagation completes. **Fix for LetsEncrypt rate limit (alejandro-villamayor case):** Switch Cloudflare SSL/TLS to "Flexible" (Dashboard → SSL/TLS → Flexible) or set the DNS record to grey cloud (DNS only) until the cert is issued, then switch back. ## Nexa Architecture — Reference for Multi-Locale Standalone Sites ``` nexa.paragu-ai.com ├── content/ │ ├── en.json (79KB — source of truth for builds) │ ├── es.json (83KB) │ ├── nl.json (82KB) │ ├── de.json (82KB) │ └── resolved.json (4KB — pre-resolved EN data for components) ├── lib/ │ └── resolve.ts (build-time content resolver — NOT used at runtime) ├── app/ │ ├── page.tsx (6 sections: hero + programs + pillars + process + testimonials + CTA) │ ├── programs/page.tsx (4 tiers with pricing, inline data) │ ├── about/page.tsx (story + 6 differentiators) │ ├── faq/page.tsx (7 accordion Q&A, inline) │ ├── blog/page.tsx (8 post cards, inline) │ ├── contact/page.tsx (Calendly + WhatsApp buttons) │ ├── [...slug]/page.tsx (catch-all for 7+ stub pages) │ └── not-found.tsx ├── components/ (header.tsx, footer.tsx, whatsapp-float.tsx) └── public/images/ (187 WebP images, 0 PNGs) ``` **Critical:** The resolver is a BUILD-TIME tool only. Content at `src/content/es.json` needs Dockerfile to copy `src/content/` and layout imports use `@/content/es.json`. 3. **Archived repos must be unarchived before push** — `gh api repos/org/repo -X PATCH -F archived=false`. Check with `gh repo view org/repo --json isArchived`. 4. **`delegate_task` subagent fails with 401**** — OpenRouter/provider auth errors prevent parallel execution. PYEOF` for batch operations. 2. **Auto-generated lead sites are TWO versions behind** — The PAB generator produces sites with an older template missing: `HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0` in Dockerfile, `traefik.docker.network=agent-net` label, healthchecks, non-root user, security headers, 404/error pages, robots/sitemap. For array, use `c.navigation || []`. 1b. **Nav hrefs may contain PAB paths (`/s/en/nexa-paraguay/programas`).** After fixing the structure (step 1a), the href values themselves are wrong — they reference old PAB routes. Fix: add `'--foreground': colors.text` right after `--accent-foreground`. ## 🔴 CRITICAL: Hero Background Overlay Must Work on Dark Themes The hero component had baked `color-mix(in srgb, var(--primary) 70%, transparent)` as the overlay gradient. For dark sites with a LIGHT primary color (dayah: `--primary: #94abd6`), this overlay is translucent light-blue — **not enough contrast** for white-ish text. The fix was two-part: 1. **Component change** (`hero-section.tsx`): Replace the baked overlay with a configurable `overlayColor` prop, defaulting to `rgba(10,10,20,0.85)`: ```ts const backgroundStyle = backgroundImage && !useResponsivePicture ? { backgroundImage: `linear-gradient(${overlayColor || 'rgba(10,10,20,0.85)'}, ${overlayColor || 'rgba(10,10,20,0.85)'}), url(${backgroundImage})` } : undefined ``` 2. **Content change** (`es.json` / `en.json`): Add `"overlayColor": "rgba(10,10,20,0.88)"` to the hero content block. Without `overlayColor` in content, the component defaults to a dark overlay. Dark sites with light `--primary` always need this. - Quote body: `lineHeight: 1.65` - Section bg: `surface-light` ### Web. Process Timeline - Connector: gradient — `linear-gradient(to bottom, var(--primary), var(--secondary))` width 1px - Card resting state: `var(--surface)` — NOT `var(--background)` (needs to pop from section bg) - Card hover: `var(--card)` + `scale-105` - CTA: `var(--secondary)` - Card width: `max-w-lg` (prevents full-width cards with short text) ## Image Assignments Service When images from `/sites/[tenant]/images/covers/` 404 but files exist in Docker: 1. **Copy to working path:** ```bash docker cp CONTAINER:/app/public/sites/tenant/images/covers/. web/public/images/tenant/covers/ ``` 2. **Update all content references:** Replace `/sites/[tenant]/images/covers/` with `/images/tenant/covers/` in es.json AND en.json 3. **The OG/favicon/brand images** at `/sites/[tenant]/images/brand/` DO serve correctly (handled by copy-tenant-images.mjs into next build output). Only `images/covers/` has the 404 issue. ## Token Components System — Per-Tenant CSS Var Overrides Add `components` key to tokens.json to emit arbitrary CSS variables: ```json { "components": { "portfolio": { "gap": "1.5rem" }, "testimonials": { "card-shadow": "0 4px 24px rgba(0,0,0,0.3)" } } } ``` → `--portfolio-gap: 1.5rem`, `--testimonials-card-shadow: ...` injected as CSS vars. This mechanism lives in `resolve-site-tokens.ts` — it iterates `merged.components` and emits each key as `--{compKey}-{propKey}` using the `propKey` kebab-cased. ## Test at Low Brightness Always check the live site at simulated 50% brightness before declaring done. What's readable at 100% often disappears at 80%. The user sees it on a phone outdoors at 40% brightness. ## Tenant Site Improvement Checklist When improving a tenant site, check in this order: 1. **Domain:** Registered? (site.json domain field vs actual DNS) 2. **Integrations:** Hubspot/Mailchimp/GA4 — real or placeholders? 3. **Pages:** Do all 6 pages render? Cloudflare proxied mode terminates TLS at the edge and connects to origin over HTTP 2. LetsEncrypt hits `http://{domain}/.well-known/acme-challenge/` through Cloudflare 3. If Cloudflare SSL/TLS mode is **"Full (strict)"**, it requires a valid cert on the origin — which doesn't exist yet 4. Result: **Error 526** — "Invalid SSL certificate" **Fix — switch Cloudflare SSL/TLS to "Flexible" temporarily:** - Cloudflare Dashboard → SSL/TLS → change from "Full (strict)" to **"Flexible"** - Cloudflare will serve HTTPS to visitors but talk HTTP to your origin - LetsEncrypt can complete the HTTP-01 challenge through port 80 - After cert is issued, switch back to "Full" or "Full (strict)" **Alternative — DNS-only (grey cloud) temporarily:** - Switch the A record from proxied (orange) to DNS-only (grey) -
